en.extract:
The flame-rumped tanager (Ramphocelus flammigerus) is a passerine bird in the tanager family Thraupidae.  It is found from Panama to western Ecuador in semi-open forest and woodland. The subspecies with a yellow rump from the Cauca Valley in Colombia, the lemon-rumped tanager, has sometimes been considered as a separate species.


Taxonomy
The flame-rumped tanager was formally described in 1833 by the English naturalists William Jardine and Prideaux John Selby based on a specimen collected on "some part of the district upon the Columbia River". They placed  the species in the genus Ramphopis and coined the binomial name Ramphopis flamigerus where the specific epithet is a misspelling of the Latin word flammigerus meaning "flame-bearing" or "fiery". It is now one of eight species placed in the genus Ramphocelus that was introduced in 1805 by the French zoologist Anselme Gaëtan Desmarest.
Two subspecies are recognised:

R. f. flammigerus (Jardine, W & Selby, PJ, 1833) – western Colombia (middle Cauca Valley southward to Nariño)
R. f. icteronotus Bonaparte, CLJL, 1838 – humid lowlands of Panama to western Colombia and western Ecuador
The subspecies R. f. icteronotus has sometimes been treated as a separate species, the lemon-rumped tanager, but a study published in 2017 found very little genetic differentiation.


Description
The flame-rumped tanager measures on average 19 cm (7.5 in) in length. It has very marked sexual dimorphism. Males of both subspecies have velvety black plumage with a scarlet-red spot on the back; the chest and rump are orange to red and the belly is bright yellow. The beak is cobalt blue to silver or whitish with a black tip. The female has an olive-brown back with dark spots; a reddish-orange rump; and a light yellow chest and belly.
The rump of the male P. f. icteronotus is yellow, while it is red in P. f. flammigerus. The rump is yellowish in the female P. f. icteronotus, while the rump, vent and chest are orange-red in P. f. flammigerus.


Distribution and habitat
The flame-rumped tanager is distributed from Panama to western Ecuador.
This species is considered quite common in its natural habitats: shrubby clearings, forest edges and gardens, mainly between 800 and 2000m above sea level.
The most widespread subspecies, icteronotus, is found in the Tumbes-Chocó-Magdalena in Panama, Colombia, Ecuador and Peru, and is sometimes considered a separate species, the lemon-rumped tanager (R.f.  icteronotus). However, it is known to hybridize with the nominate subspecies from the Cauca Valley in Colombia.

The '''flame-rumped tanager''' ('''''Ramphocelus flammigerus''''') is a [[passerine]] bird in the tanager family [[Thraupidae]].  It is found from Panama to western Ecuador in semi-open forest and woodland. The subspecies with a yellow rump from the [[Cauca River|Cauca Valley]] in Colombia, the lemon-rumped tanager, has sometimes been considered as a separate species.

==Taxonomy==
The flame-rumped tanager was [[species description|formally described]] in 1833 by the English naturalists [[William Jardine (naturalist)|William Jardine]] and [[Prideaux John Selby]] based on a specimen collected on "some part of the district upon the Columbia River". They placed  the species in the genus ''Ramphopis'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Ramphopis flamigerus'' where the specific epithet is a misspelling of the [[Latin]] word ''flammigerus'' meaning "flame-bearing" or "fiery".<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Jardine | first1=William | author1-link=William Jardine (naturalist) | last2=Selby | first2=Prideaux John | author2-link=Prideaux John Selby | date=1833 | title=Illustrations of Ornithology | volume=3 | location=Edinburgh | publisher=W.H. Lizars | at=Plate 131, text | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/63405716 }} Issued in parts. For the publication date see: {{ cite journal | last=Zimmer | first=John T. | author-link=John T. Zimmer | year=1926 | title=Catalogue of the Edward E. Ayer Ornithological Library | journal=Field Museum of Natural History, Zoology Series | volume=16 | issue=1 | pages=322-324 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36194975 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1970 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=13 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=316 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14483551 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=flammigerus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=flammigerus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=29 April 2026 }}</ref> It is now one of eight species placed in the genus ''[[Ramphocelus]]'' that was introduced in 1805 by the French zoologist [[Anselme Gaëtan Desmarest]].<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=29 April 2026 }}</ref>

Two [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist/>
* ''R. f. flammigerus'' ([[Sir William Jardine, 7th Baronet|Jardine, W]] & [[Prideaux John Selby|Selby, PJ]], 1833) – western Colombia (middle [[Cauca River|Cauca Valley]] southward to [[Nariño Department|Nariño]])
* ''R. f. icteronotus'' [[Charles Lucien Bonaparte|Bonaparte, CLJL]], 1838 – humid lowlands of Panama to western Colombia and western Ecuador

The subspecies ''R. f. icteronotus'' has sometimes been treated as a separate species, the lemon-rumped tanager, but a study published in 2017 found very little genetic differentiation.<ref name=avilist/><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Morales-Rozo | first1=A. | last2=Tenorio | first2=E.A. | last3=Carling | first3=M.D. | last4=Cadena | first4=C.D. | date=2017 | title=Origin and cross-century dynamics of an avian hybrid zone | journal=BMC Evolutionary Biology | volume=17 | issue=1 | pages=257 | doi=10.1186/s12862-017-1096-7 | doi-access=free | pmc=5732383 }}</ref>

== Description ==
The flame-rumped tanager measures on average {{cvt|19|cm}} in length. It has very marked [[sexual dimorphism]]. Males of both subspecies have velvety black [[plumage]] with a scarlet-red spot on the back; the chest and rump are orange to red and the belly is bright yellow. The beak is cobalt blue to silver or whitish with a black tip. The female has an olive-brown back with dark spots; a reddish-orange rump; and a light yellow chest and belly.<ref name="Hilty">{{cite book |last=Hilty|first=Steven L.|date=1986|page=624|title=A Guide to the Birds of Colombia|publisher= [[Princeton University Press]] }}</ref>

The rump of the male ''P. f. icteronotus'' is yellow, while it is red in ''P. f. flammigerus''. The rump is yellowish in the female ''P. f. icteronotus'', while the rump, vent and chest are orange-red in ''P. f. flammigerus''.

== Distribution and habitat ==
The flame-rumped tanager is distributed from Panama to western Ecuador.<ref name=iucn/><ref name=RR/>

This species is considered quite common in its natural habitats: shrubby clearings, forest edges and gardens, mainly between 800 and 2000m above sea level.<ref name="RR">{{cite book |last=Ridgely|first=Robert|last2=Tudor|first2=Guy|date=2009|page=''Ramphocelus flammigerus'', p. 614, lámina 101(10)|title=Field guide to the songbirds of South America: the passerines|edition=1<sup>a.</sup>|series=Mildred Wyatt-World series in ornithology|location=University of Texas Press|publisher=Austin|isbn= 978-0-292-71748-0}}</ref>

The most widespread [[subspecies]], ''icteronotus'', is found in the [[Tumbes-Chocó-Magdalena]] in [[Panama]], [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]] and [[Peru]]<!--recently discovered in Tumbes in Peru-->, and is sometimes considered a separate species, the lemon-rumped tanager (''R.f.  icteronotus''). However, it is known to [[Hybrid (biology)|hybridize]] with the [[nominate subspecies]] from the [[Cauca River|Cauca Valley]] in Colombia.
